rationale: The citing paper explicitly uses Shiv, Carmon, and Ariely (2005) in its theoretical discussion of placebo effects, summarizing it as a core account of expectancy and conditioning-based marketing placebo mechanisms (especially price). It then interprets its own findings against that backdrop, arguing that self-expressive customization can improve performance through identity-related motivation independent of product efficacy beliefs. This is more than a passing mention because the cited work helps frame how the authors position their contribution relative to established placebo explanations.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Shiv et al.'s marketing placebo framework by contrasting its price/expectancy pathway with an identity-extension pathway from self-expressive customization. It extends the prior line by proposing that performance effects can occur without changes in efficacy beliefs.
